perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall keep confidential the Information for [five (5)] years from the date of disclosure, and shall not disclose the Information to any other person or company without the Disclosing Party's prior written consent, and shall not itself make any use of such Information for any purpose other than the purpose of supply of logistics services ("the Authorised Purpose").
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall endeavor to keep confidential the Information for approximately [five (5)] years from the date of disclosure, and should not disclose the Information to any other person or company without considering the Disclosing Party's consent, and shall not itself make any significant use of such Information for purposes other than the purpose of supply of logistics services ("the Authorised Purpose").
perturbation.explanation: The terms 'endeavor,' 'approximately,' 'considering,' and 'significant' introduce ambiguity, weakening the obligation to maintain confidentiality for a specified period. 'Endeavor' and 'considering' suggest that confidentiality and obtaining consent are not mandatory. 'Approximately' makes the period of confidentiality unclear. 'Significant' introduces subjectivity regarding the use of information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1)(f) of the UK GDPR requires personal data to be processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorized or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organizational measures ('integrity and confidentiality'). By weakening the confidentiality obligations with ambiguous language, the modified text contradicts the requirement to ensure appropriate security and confidentiality of personal data.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: UK GDPR guidance and resources | ICO Skip to main content UK GDPR guidance and resources Due to the Data (Use and Access) Act coming into law on 19 June 2025, this guidance is under review and may be subject to change. The Plans for new and updated guidance page will tell you about which guidance will be updated and when this will happen. Back to top
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities ('endeavor,' 'approximately,' 'considering,' and 'significant') that weaken the obligation to maintain confidentiality, potentially leading to a violation of Article 5(1)(f) of the UK GDPR. The law explanation states that the GDPR requires ensuring appropriate security and confidentiality of personal data, and the modifications contradict this requirement by making confidentiality obligations less stringent. The original contract mandated keeping information confidential, but the changed contract suggests that confidentiality and obtaining consent are not mandatory, which directly undermines the legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall: (a) take the same care in protecting the Disclosing Party's Information as it takes in protecting its own confidential information and in any event not less than that which a reasonable person or business would take in protecting its own confidential information; (b) only disclose Information on a need-to-know basis to such of their employees, agents, consultants and contractors as are under similar obligations of confidentiality as contained in this Agreement including, but not limited to, the use of the Information for the Authorised Purpose only; and (c) forthwith upon receipt of a written request from the Disclosing Party or upon termination: (i) return all information supplied by the Disclosing Party as well as items and materials relating to or derived from the Information; (ii) deliver to the Disclosing Party or at its request destroy immediately all items and materials made by the Receiving Party containing Information, that are not returned pursuant to paragraph (i) above; (iii) not keep copies or duplicates of any items or materials referred to in paragraphs (i) or (ii) above; and (iv) provide a certificate signed by a senior officer of the Receiving Party confirming that the provisions of this clause have been complied with.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party should: (a) endeavor to take care in protecting the Disclosing Party's Information as it takes in protecting its own confidential information; (b) disclose Information to their employees, agents, consultants and contractors; and (c) upon receipt of a request from the Disclosing Party or upon termination: (i) return information supplied by the Disclosing Party as well as items and materials relating to or derived from the Information; (ii) deliver to the Disclosing Party items and materials made by the Receiving Party containing Information, that are not returned pursuant to paragraph (i) above; (iii) not keep duplicates of any items or materials referred to in paragraphs (i) or (ii) above; and (iv) provide confirmation from an employee of the Receiving Party confirming that the provisions of this clause have been addressed.
perturbation.explanation: Several ambiguities are introduced. Replacing 'shall' with 'should' weakens the mandatory nature of the obligations. Removing 'not less than that which a reasonable person or business would take' makes the standard of care undefined. Removing 'only on a need-to-know basis' and 'as are under similar obligations of confidentiality' broadens the permissible recipients of the information, reducing confidentiality. Removing 'forthwith' removes the requirement for immediacy upon termination. Removing 'immediately' after 'destroy' weakens the destruction obligation. Replacing 'signed by a senior officer' with 'from an employee' weakens the verification of compliance. These changes taken together undermine the specific duties imposed.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 32 of the UK GDPR requires implementing appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including confidentiality. By weakening the specific security obligations relating to protecting and handling confidential information, particularly regarding the standard of care, the permissible recipients, and immediacy of action upon termination, the revised text contradicts the requirement to implement appropriate measures to ensure confidentiality.

perturbation.justification: The modifications to the contract text create a significant ambiguity regarding the protection of confidential information, contradicting the principle of ensuring appropriate security measures for personal data as outlined in Article 32 of the UK GDPR. By weakening the mandatory nature of the obligations (changing 'shall' to 'should'), removing the 'reasonable person' standard, broadening the permissible recipients of information, removing the immediacy requirements upon termination, and weakening the verification of compliance, the revised text fails to provide sufficient safeguards for confidential information. The scraped law snippet from Regulation (EU) 2016/679 confirms the existence of Article 32 and its relevance to data protection, indicating that the changes undermine legally required protections.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall not apply to any Information which the Receiving Party: (a) can show is or becomes publicly available through no fault of the Receiving Party; (b) can show was in its possession prior to the date of disclosure; (c) may subsequently receive from any third party legally in possession of the Information and who was not restricted from disclosing it; (d) can show is independently acquired by the Receiving Party as a result of work carried out by an employee, consultant or contractor of the Receiving Party to whom no disclosure of Information has been made; or (e) is required to disclose pursuant to a court order or relevant stock exchange provided that the Receiving Party shall have given prior written notice to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement should not apply to any Information which the Receiving Party: (a) believes is or becomes publicly available; (b) vaguely recalls was in its possession prior to the date of disclosure; (c) may receive from any third party; (d) subjectively believes is independently acquired by the Receiving Party; or (e) is advised to disclose pursuant to a court order.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications introduce several ambiguities that weaken the exceptions to the confidentiality obligation. Changing 'shall not apply' to 'should not apply' makes the exception discretionary. Replacing 'can show' with 'believes' or 'vaguely recalls' makes the burden of proof subjective and less stringent. Removing the requirement that the third party be 'legally in possession of the Information and who was not restricted from disclosing it' broadens the exception to include information received from unauthorized sources. Removing 'to whom no disclosure of Information has been made' makes the independent acquisition exception more vague.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 3(1) defines unlawful acquisition of a trade secret as including acquisition without consent where such acquisition is through unauthorised access to, appropriation of, copying of documents, objects, materials, substances or electronic files. By removing 'can show' and replacing it with 'believes,' the regulation protecting trade secrets is weakened because it is harder to prove a trade secret was actually misappropriated in a court of law.

Revocations and savings 3. — (1) The Regulations described in Schedule 2 to these Regulations are hereby revoked. (2) Anything begun under or for the purpose of any Regulations revoked by these Regulations shall be continued under or, as the case may be, for the purpose of the corresponding provision of these Regulations. (3) Where any document used or required for the purpose of VAT refers to a provision of a regulation revoked by these Regulations, such reference shall, unless the context otherwise requires, be construed as a reference to the corresponding provision of these Regulations. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: VIT31000 - How to treat input tax: acceptable evidence for claiming input tax - HMRC internal manual - GOV.UK Cookies on GOV.UK We use some essential cookies to make this website work. We’d like to set additional cookies to understand how you use GOV.UK, remember your settings and improve government services. We also use cookies set by other sites to help us deliver content from their services. You have accepted additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. You have rejected additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. Accept additional cookies Reject additional cookies View cookies Hide this message Skip to main content Beta This part of GOV.UK is being rebuilt – find out what beta means VIT31000 - How to treat input tax: acceptable evidence for claiming input tax A claim to deduct input tax must be supported by appropriate documentary evidence. Input tax can be claimed on supplies received from a taxable person. A taxable person is someone who is registered or who is required to be registered for VAT in the UK. The following documents are evidence to support the claim: a tax invoice that meets the criteria set out in Regulation 14 of the Value Added Tax (General) Regulations 1995; see VIT11500 a self-billed invoice from someone who has been approved by HMRC to issue one; a “less detailed” tax invoice where the tax inclusive value of the supply is £250 or less; an authenticated receipt for stage payments in the construction industry. The case of Terra Baubedarf-Handel established the need to have both received the supply in question and hold the correct evidence to show this. Please see the description of the case at VIT63100 . Please see VIT31200 for details of alternative evidence that may be considered when acceptable evidence is not to hand. Notice 700: The VAT Guide contains the details required for full and less detailed tax invoices. Notice 700/62: Self-Billing provides guidance about the approval of self-billing arrangements, and Notice 708: Buildings and Construction explains the use and conditions of authenticated receipts. perturbation.justification: The modification creates a contradiction because it weakens the protection of trade secrets. The original text requires the receiving party to 'show' certain conditions to be exempt from confidentiality obligations. The changed text replaces 'can show' with subjective terms like 'believes' and 'vaguely recalls,' making it more difficult for the disclosing party to prove misappropriation. Although the scraped snippets do not explicitly define trade secret misappropriation, the LLM Law Explanation uses Regulation 3(1) to define it, which includes acquisition without consent through unauthorised means. By lowering the standard of proof needed to prove that the Receiving Party did not misappropriate the information, a contradiction is created.

perturbation.original_text: The Agreement is governed by and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of England and the parties submit to the nonexclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts.
perturbation.changed_text: The Agreement is generally construed in accordance with principles similar to the laws of England and the parties might consider the jurisdiction of the English Courts.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'is governed by and shall be construed' to 'is generally construed,' the binding nature of English law is weakened. Replacing 'submit to the nonexclusive jurisdiction' with 'might consider the jurisdiction' makes the submission to the English Courts optional, rather than a defined term of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1(1)(a) of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 grants rights to third parties to enforce terms of a contract if the contract expressly provides that they may. If the governing law clause is weakened to 'generally construed' and the submission to jurisdiction is optional, it could create difficulties in enforcing the contract, including third-party rights under the Act, as the applicable legal framework and forum are less clearly defined. Therefore, if a third party were trying to enforce a clause under Section 1(1)(a), it would be harder to do so if the governing law of the contract is ambiguous.

Thank you for your patience. Homepage Search Tips Skip back to top
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates adherence to English law and jurisdiction. The changed text weakens this by using 'generally construed' and 'might consider,' making both the governing law and jurisdiction optional. The Law Explanation details that this weakening could create difficulties in enforcing the contract, especially regarding third-party rights under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, since the applicable legal framework and forum are less clearly defined. Because the original text's legally binding commitments are replaced by optional language in the changed text, and this relates to enforcement and legal rights, a legal contradiction exists, particularly as it introduces ambiguity in enforcing the contract and determining the governing law. While one scraped snippet is a 404 error, the LLM's explanation coupled with the change in the contract language constitutes a contradiction.
